ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic hot pot food product class; no Vietnamese-specific beef hotpot entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Beef and vegetable soup/stew — used as closest FoodEx2 analogue for a broth-based beef hotpot dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code for Vietnamese Lau Bo exists. A0CKW (beef and vegetable soup/stew) is the closest defensible analogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Vietnamese beef hotpot as a specific food concept.

serving.description: 1 individual bowl serving from shared hotpot
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical individual serving drawn from a shared Lau Bo hotpot is approximately 400 g, comprising broth, beef slices, vegetables, tofu, and a portion of noodles.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Soup, beef and vegetables, canned, ready-to-serve (FDC ID 172163) and Beef, chuck, arm pot roast, separable lean only, cooked, braised (FDC ID 174032); composite adapted estimate for Vietnamese beef hotpot (Lau Bo)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Vietnamese Lau Bo exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for beef and vegetable soup (FDC ID 172163) and cooked beef (FDC ID 174032), cross-referenced with the input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7). Input values for energy (120 kcal/100g), protein (8 g), fat (6 g), carbohydrate (8 g), sodium (300 mg), dietary fibre (1 g), saturated fat (2.33 g), sugar (1 g), calcium (16.67 mg), iron (1.0 mg), cholesterol (26.67 mg), trans fat (0.1 g), and vitamin C (2.67 mg) are broadly consistent with USDA beef and vegetable soup/stew data for a broth-diluted mixed dish. Values retained with medium confidence and documented as adapted estimates. The input source URL (foodo.sg) was not used as a primary nutrient source as it did not contain a direct Lau Bo nutrient entry; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy is used as the approved secondary global source per Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021: a systematic review. American Journal of Clinical Nutrition. 2021;114(5):1625-1632. Rice vermicelli GI reference used as primary carbohydrate analogue for Lau Bo.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Vietnamese Lau Bo in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. GI estimated at 52 based on the composite nature of the dish: rice vermicelli (GI approximately 53, per Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables, University of Sydney GI Database), beef (GI effectively 0, negligible carbohydrate contribution), non-starchy vegetables (low GI), and broth (low GI). A weighted composite GI of approximately 52 is defensible for a mixed hotpot dish where rice vermicelli is the primary carbohydrate source. Glycemic load per 400 g individual serving calculated as: (52 × 32 g available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 16.6. This is an analogous estimate and should be replaced with a direct measurement if one becomes available in an approved GI source.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 300 mg per 100 g from fish sauce and beef broth; a 400 g serving delivers approximately 1200 mg sodium, representing 52–60% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid drinking the broth, request reduced fish sauce during preparation, and increase the proportion of low-sodium vegetables such as watercress, cabbage, and mushrooms to partially offset sodium load with potassium and dietary fibre.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate at 8 g per 100 g with low dietary fibre (1 g per 100 g); a 400 g serving provides approximately 32 g carbohydrate with only 4 g fibre, yielding a glycemic load of approximately 16.6, which is moderate.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should substitute rice vermicelli with shirataki noodles or increase the proportion of tofu and non-starchy vegetables to reduce glycemic load, and avoid high-GI additions such as taro and corn.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 2.33 g per 100 g and cholesterol at 26.67 mg per 100 g from beef slices; trans fat at 0.1 g per 100 g; sodium at 300 mg per 100 g compounds cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size of beef slices, avoid drinking the broth to reduce sodium and saturated fat intake, and substitute a portion of beef with tofu or mushrooms to lower saturated fat and cholesterol load per serving.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef is red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acids), which promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine production; chilli is a nightshade vegetable that may trigger flares in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should consider substituting beef slices with fish, tofu, or mushrooms as the primary protein, monitor individual tolerance to chilli, and increase the proportion of ginger and mushrooms for their anti-inflammatory properties.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef Hotpot (Lau Bo), known in Vietnamese as Lẩu Bò, is a traditional Vietnamese communal dining dish in which thinly sliced beef and an assortment of vegetables, tofu, and mushrooms are cooked tableside in a simmering, aromatic broth. The broth is typically seasoned with lemongrass, ginger, fish sauce, and chilli, producing a fragrant, savoury, and mildly spicy base. Diners cook their own ingredients in the shared pot and consume them with rice vermicelli or glass noodles. The dish is popular in Vietnam and widely served in Vietnamese restaurants across Southeast Asia, including Singapore. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 120 kcal, 8 g protein, 6 g fat (2.33 g saturated), 8 g carbohydrate, 1 g dietary fibre, and 300 mg sodium. The moderate protein content comes primarily from beef and tofu, while the carbohydrate load is driven by noodles and starchy vegetables such as taro and corn. Sodium is elevated due to fish sauce and broth, making this dish a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 52 (low-moderate), with a glycemic load of approximately 16.6 per 400 g individual serving. Clinically, the dish is moderate concern for type 2 diabetes due to refined carbohydrate from noodles and low fibre, high concern for hypertension due to sodium load, and moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat and cholesterol from beef. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans. It may contain soy (tofu), fish (fish sauce), and gluten (if wheat noodles are used).

Is Lau Bo suitable for diabetics?
What is the sodium content of Vietnamese beef hotpot?

Can I eat Lau Bo if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Vietnamese Lau Bo (Beef Hotpot) was found in any primary approved regional source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy beef and vegetable soup/stew entries, cross-referenced with the input record (ai_generated, confidence 0.7). GI is an analogous estimate based on rice vermicelli GI from the University of Sydney GI Database per Atkinson et al. 2021. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite nature of the dish and absence of a direct approved-source match.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite Vietnamese beef hotpot dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or any other approved source at the level of a prepared mixed hotpot dish as of 2026-04-17. Trans fat (0.1 g per 100 g) is retained from the input record as a plausible trace value for a beef-containing dish; consistent with USDA beef data for cooked beef products. All core macros (protein_g 8.0, fat_g 6.0, carbohydrate_g 8.0) are non-zero and supported by adapted USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy source data; no zero-macro justification is required. The input source listing (foodo.sg URL) was not used as a primary nutrient source as it did not contain a direct Lau Bo nutrient entry and is a delivery-platform-adjacent listing; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy was used as the approved secondary global source per Project Veritas v2 source policy. GI estimated at 52 using rice vermicelli as the primary carbohydrate reference from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2021, Am J Clin Nutr); this is an analogous estimate and should be replaced with a direct measurement if one becomes available in an approved GI source.
